State the precise (ε–δ) definition of lim_{x→a} f(x) = L.
column2: For every ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that if 0 < |x − a| < δ then |f(x) − L| < ε.

column1: When is f continuous at x = a?
column2: f is continuous at a if f(a) is defined, lim_{x→a} f(x) exists, and lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a).

column1: Evaluate lim_{x→0} (sin x)/x.
State the limit definition of the derivative f'(x).
Product rule: d/dx[f(x)g(x)] = ?
column2: f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)

column1: Quotient rule: d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = ?
Chain rule: d/dx[f(g(x))] = ?
d/dx[tan x] = ?
column2: sec^2 x

column1: d/dx[ln x] = ?
d/dx[e^x] = ?
column2: e^x

column1: d/dx[arctan x] = ?
d/dx[arcsin x] = ?
State the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1).
column2: If f is continuous on [a,b] and F(x) = ∫_a^x f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(x).

column1: State the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 2, evaluation).
column2: If f is continuous on [a,b] and F is any antiderivative of f, then ∫_a^b f(x) dx = F(b) − F(a).

column1: Integration by parts formula: ∫ u dv = ?
column2: uv − ∫ v du
ln|x| + C

column1: ∫ sec^2 x dx = ?
column2: tan x + C
arctan x + C

column1: ∫ e^x dx = ?
column2: e^x + C

column1: Evaluate ∫ x e^x dx.
column2: x e^x − e^x + C

column1: Sum of a convergent geometric series Σ_{n=0}^∞ a r^n (|r| < 1) = ?
For which p does the p-series Σ_{n=1}^∞ 1/n^p converge?
column2: It converges if and only if p > 1.

column1: State the Ratio Test conclusion using L = lim_{n→∞} |a_{n+1}/a_n|.
column2: If L < 1 the series converges absolutely; if L > 1 (or ∞) it diverges; if L = 1 the test is inconclusive.

column1: State the Alternating Series Test.
column2: Σ (−1)^n b_n converges if b_n > 0, b_n is decreasing, and lim_{n→∞} b_n = 0.

column1: Maclaurin series of e^x = ?
Maclaurin series of sin x = ?
Maclaurin series of cos x = ?
Power series of 1/(1 − x) for |x| < 1 = ?
Define the gradient ∇f of a scalar function f(x,y,z).
column2: ∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z), the vector of first partial derivatives.

column1: For z = f(x,y) with x = x(t), y = y(t), the chain rule gives dz/dt = ?
column2: dz/dt = (∂f/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂f/∂y)(dy/dt)

column1: Give the area element dA for a double integral in polar coordinates.
column2: dA = r dr dθ

column1: State the Jacobian volume element for spherical coordinates (ρ, φ, θ).
column2: dV = ρ^2 sin φ dρ dφ dθ

column1: State Green's Theorem.
column2: ∮_C (P dx + Q dy) = ∬_D (∂Q/∂x − ∂P/∂y) dA, where C is the positively oriented boundary of region D.

column1: State the Divergence Theorem.
column2: ∬_S F · dS = ∭_V (∇ · F) dV, where S is the closed boundary surface of solid region V.

column1: State Stokes' Theorem.
column2: ∮_C F · dr = ∬_S (∇ × F) · dS, where C is the boundary of surface S with compatible orientation.

column1: Give the arc length of y = f(x) from x = a to x = b.
Volume of revolution about the x-axis (disk method) for y = f(x), a ≤ x ≤ b = ?
column2: V = π ∫_a^b (f(x))^2 dx

column1: Solve the separable ODE dy/dx = k y.
column2: y = C e^(k x)

column1: Give the integrating factor for the linear ODE y' + P(x) y = Q(x).
column2: μ(x) = e^(∫ P(x) dx)

column1: For a 2x2 matrix A = [[a, b], [c, d]], what is det(A)?
column2: det(A) = ad − bc

column1: How do you expand det of a 3x3 matrix [[a,b,c],[d,e,f],[g,h,i]] along the first row?
column2: det = a(ei − fh) − b(di − fg) + c(dh − eg)

column1: When is a square matrix A invertible? Give several equivalent conditions.
column2: A is invertible ⇔ det(A) ≠ 0 ⇔ rank = n (full rank) ⇔ columns are linearly independent ⇔ 0 is not an eigenvalue ⇔ Ax = 0 has only the trivial solution

column1: State the rank-nullity theorem for a linear map T: V → W with V finite-dimensional.
column2: dim(V) = rank(T) + nullity(T), i.e. dim(V) = dim(im T) + dim(ker T)

column1: What is a basis of a vector space, and what is the dimension?
column2: A basis is a linearly independent set that spans the space; the dimension is the number of vectors in any basis (all bases have the same size)

column1: Define an eigenvalue and eigenvector of a matrix A.
column2: A nonzero vector v with Av = λv for a scalar λ; λ is the eigenvalue and v is an eigenvector

column1: How is the characteristic polynomial of an n×n matrix A defined?
column2: p(λ) = det(A − λI); its roots are the eigenvalues of A

column1: When is an n×n matrix diagonalizable (over a field)?
column2: A is diagonalizable ⇔ it has n linearly independent eigenvectors ⇔ for every eigenvalue its geometric multiplicity equals its algebraic multiplicity

column1: Relate trace and determinant of A to its eigenvalues (with multiplicity).
column2: trace(A) = sum of eigenvalues; det(A) = product of eigenvalues

column1: Is matrix multiplication commutative? State the rule for when AB is defined.
column2: No, in general AB ≠ BA; AB is defined when A is m×n and B is n×p, giving an m×p matrix

column1: How do elementary row operations affect a determinant?
column2: Swapping two rows multiplies det by −1; scaling a row by k multiplies det by k; adding a multiple of one row to another leaves det unchanged

column1: When are two vectors u and v orthogonal?
column2: When their dot product is zero: u · v = 0

column1: Give the cross product formula for a = (a1,a2,a3) and b = (b1,b2,b3) in R^3.
column2: a × b = (a2 b3 − a3 b2, a3 b1 − a1 b3, a1 b2 − a2 b1); it is orthogonal to both a and b

column1: Give the dot product formula and its geometric meaning.
column2: u · v = u1 v1 + u2 v2 + ... + un vn = |u| |v| cos θ, where θ is the angle between u and v

column1: What is the idea behind the Gram-Schmidt process?
column2: It converts a linearly independent set into an orthogonal (or orthonormal) basis by subtracting from each vector its projections onto the previously constructed vectors

column1: For Ax = b, how do rank(A) and rank([A|b]) determine solvability?
column2: Consistent ⇔ rank(A) = rank([A|b]); then unique solution if rank = number of unknowns, infinitely many if rank < number of unknowns

column1: What three properties must a set G with operation · satisfy to be a group?
column2: Associativity, existence of an identity element e, and existence of an inverse for every element (closure under · is also assumed)

column1: Define the order of a group and the order of an element g.
column2: The order of a group is its number of elements |G|; the order of g is the smallest positive integer n with g^n = e (∞ if none exists)

column1: State the subgroup test for a nonempty subset H of a group G.
column2: H ≤ G ⇔ H is nonempty and for all a, b ∈ H, ab^(−1) ∈ H

column1: State Lagrange's theorem.
column2: If G is a finite group and H ≤ G, then |H| divides |G|; in particular the order of any element divides |G|

column1: What is a cyclic group?
column2: A group generated by a single element: G = ⟨g⟩ = {g^k : k ∈ Z}; every cyclic group is abelian

column1: What is a (left) coset of a subgroup H in G?
column2: For g ∈ G, the set gH = {gh : h ∈ H}; the distinct left cosets partition G into pieces of equal size |H|

column1: When is a subgroup N of G normal?
column2: N ⊴ G ⇔ gNg^(−1) = N for all g ∈ G (equivalently gN = Ng for all g)

column1: What is the quotient group G/N, and what condition on N is needed?
column2: When N ⊴ G, G/N is the group of cosets with (aN)(bN) = (ab)N; N must be normal for this to be well-defined

column1: Define a group homomorphism φ: G → H.
column2: A map with φ(ab) = φ(a)φ(b) for all a, b ∈ G; it sends identity to identity and inverses to inverses

column1: What is a group isomorphism?
column2: A bijective homomorphism; if one exists, G and H are isomorphic (G ≅ H) and share all group-theoretic properties

column1: Define the kernel of a homomorphism φ: G → H, and its key properties.
column2: ker(φ) = {g ∈ G : φ(g) = e_H}; it is a normal subgroup of G, and φ is injective ⇔ ker(φ) = {e}

column1: What is the symmetric group S_n and its order?
column2: S_n is the group of all bijections (permutations) of {1,...,n} under composition; |S_n| = n!

column1: What determines the parity (even/odd) of a permutation?
column2: The parity of the number of transpositions in any decomposition; even permutations form the alternating group A_n of order n!/2

column1: What are the defining properties of a ring (with unity)?
column2: (R, +) is an abelian group, multiplication is associative and distributes over addition, and there is a multiplicative identity 1

column1: Define an ideal I of a commutative ring R.
column2: A subgroup (I, +) such that for all r ∈ R and a ∈ I, ra ∈ I (absorbs multiplication by ring elements)

column1: What is an integral domain?
column2: A commutative ring with 1 ≠ 0 that has no zero divisors: ab = 0 ⇒ a = 0 or b = 0

column1: What is a field?
column2: A commutative ring with 1 ≠ 0 in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse

column1: For which n is the ring Z/nZ a field?
column2: Exactly when n is prime; Z/nZ is an integral domain ⇔ n is prime, and then it is also a field

column1: What does a | b (a divides b) mean for integers?
column2: There exists an integer k with b = ak (and a ≠ 0)

column1: State the relationship between gcd and lcm of positive integers a and b.
column2: gcd(a,b) · lcm(a,b) = a · b

column1: How does the Euclidean algorithm compute gcd(a, b)?
column2: Repeatedly replace (a, b) by (b, a mod b) until the remainder is 0; the last nonzero remainder is gcd(a, b)

column1: What does a ≡ b (mod n) mean?
column2: n divides (a − b); equivalently a and b leave the same remainder upon division by n

column1: State Fermat's little theorem.
column2: If p is prime and gcd(a, p) = 1, then a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p); for any a, a^p ≡ a (mod p)

column1: Define Euler's totient function φ(n) and give φ for a prime p and a prime power p^k.
column2: φ(n) counts integers in {1,...,n} coprime to n; φ(p) = p − 1 and φ(p^k) = p^k − p^(k−1)

column1: State Euler's theorem.
column2: If gcd(a, n) = 1, then a^(φ(n)) ≡ 1 (mod n)

column1: State the Chinese Remainder Theorem for two moduli.
column2: If gcd(m, n) = 1, the system x ≡ a (mod m), x ≡ b (mod n) has a unique solution modulo mn

column1: State the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
column2: Every integer n > 1 factors into primes uniquely up to the order of the factors

column1: Compute 7^100 mod 5.
Compute 2^10 mod 11 using Fermat's little theorem.
column2: By Fermat, 2^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11) since 11 is prime and gcd(2,11) = 1

column1: State the ε–δ definition of lim(x→a) f(x) = L.
column2: For every ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that for all x, if 0 < |x − a| < δ then |f(x) − L| < ε.

column1: State the ε–δ definition of continuity of f at a point a.
column2: f is continuous at a if for every ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that for all x, |x − a| < δ implies |f(x) − f(a)| < ε.

column1: What does it mean for a sequence (aₙ) to converge to L?
column2: For every ε > 0 there exists N such that for all n ≥ N, |aₙ − L| < ε.

column1: Define a Cauchy sequence.
column2: (aₙ) is Cauchy if for every ε > 0 there exists N such that for all m, n ≥ N, |aₘ − aₙ| < ε.

column1: What is meant by completeness of ℝ, and how does it relate to Cauchy sequences?
column2: ℝ is complete: every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges to a limit in ℝ. Equivalently, every nonempty set bounded above has a least upper bound.

column1: State the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem.
column2: Every bounded sequence in ℝ (or ℝⁿ) has a convergent subsequence.

column1: Define the supremum (sup) of a nonempty set S ⊆ ℝ that is bounded above.
column2: sup S is the least upper bound of S: it is an upper bound M such that no number smaller than M is an upper bound. That is, M is an upper bound and for every ε > 0 there is s ∈ S with s > M − ε.

column1: Distinguish pointwise from uniform convergence of fₙ → f on a set E.
column2: Pointwise: for each x ∈ E and each ε > 0 there is N (depending on x) with |fₙ(x) − f(x)| < ε for n ≥ N. Uniform: N can be chosen independent of x, so sup over x of |fₙ(x) − f(x)| → 0.

column1: State the nth-term test (divergence test) for a series Σ aₙ.
column2: If Σ aₙ converges then aₙ → 0. Contrapositive: if aₙ does not tend to 0, the series diverges. (The converse fails, e.g. Σ 1/n.)

column1: State the Intermediate Value Theorem.
column2: If f is continuous on [a, b] and y is any value between f(a) and f(b), then there exists c ∈ [a, b] with f(c) = y.

column1: State the Extreme Value Theorem.
column2: If f is continuous on a closed bounded interval [a, b], then f attains a maximum and a minimum value on [a, b].

column1: State the Mean Value Theorem.
column2: If f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists c ∈ (a, b) with f′(c) = (f(b) − f(a))/(b − a).

column1: Is differentiability at a point stronger or weaker than continuity there? Give the implication.
column2: If f is differentiable at a, then f is continuous at a. The converse is false (e.g. f(x) = |x| at 0 is continuous but not differentiable).

column1: State the Monotone Convergence Theorem for sequences.
column2: A monotone (increasing or decreasing) sequence of real numbers converges if and only if it is bounded; an increasing bounded sequence converges to its supremum.

column1: Give a criterion for Riemann integrability on [a, b] in terms of upper and lower sums.
column2: A bounded f is Riemann integrable on [a, b] iff for every ε > 0 there is a partition P with U(f, P) − L(f, P) < ε (upper and lower Darboux sums differ by less than ε).

column1: Define an open set in a topological space.
column2: A set U is open if U belongs to the topology; equivalently in a metric space, for every point x ∈ U there is r > 0 with the open ball B(x, r) ⊆ U.

column1: Define a closed set in a topological space.
column2: A set is closed if its complement is open. Equivalently, it contains all of its limit points.

column1: Define the interior, closure, and boundary of a set A.
column2: Interior: largest open set contained in A (union of all open subsets). Closure: smallest closed set containing A (intersection of all closed supersets). Boundary: closure(A) ∩ closure(complement of A), i.e. closure minus interior.

column1: Define a basis for a topology on X.
column2: A collection B of open sets is a basis if every open set is a union of members of B; equivalently, for each x and open U containing x there is B ∈ 𝓑 with x ∈ B ⊆ U.

column1: Give the topological characterization of continuity of f : X → Y.
column2: f is continuous iff the preimage f⁻¹(V) is open in X for every open set V ⊆ Y (equivalently, preimages of closed sets are closed).

column1: Define compactness of a topological space.
column2: A space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover: for any collection of open sets whose union is X, finitely many of them already cover X.

column1: State the Heine–Borel theorem in ℝⁿ.
column2: A subset of ℝⁿ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

column1: Define a connected topological space.
column2: X is connected if it cannot be written as the union of two disjoint nonempty open sets; equivalently, the only subsets that are both open and closed are ∅ and X.

column1: Define a Hausdorff (T₂) space.
column2: A space is Hausdorff if any two distinct points x ≠ y have disjoint open neighborhoods U ∋ x and V ∋ y with U ∩ V = ∅.

column1: Define a metric space.
column2: A set X with a function d : X × X → [0, ∞) such that d(x, y) = 0 iff x = y, d(x, y) = d(y, x) (symmetry), and d(x, z) ≤ d(x, y) + d(y, z) (triangle inequality).

column1: Define a homeomorphism between topological spaces.
column2: A bijection f : X → Y such that both f and f⁻¹ are continuous. If one exists, X and Y are homeomorphic (topologically equivalent).

column1: Is the open interval (0, 1) compact in ℝ? Justify briefly.
column2: No. It is bounded but not closed, so by Heine–Borel it is not compact. E.g. the cover {(1/n, 1) : n ≥ 2} has no finite subcover.

column1: Define a complex number's modulus |z| and argument arg(z) for z = x + iy.
column2: |z| = √(x² + y²), the distance from 0. arg(z) is the angle θ with x = |z|cos θ, y = |z|sin θ, so z = |z|(cos θ + i sin θ).

column1: State Euler's formula.
column2: e^{iθ} = cos θ + i sin θ. In particular e^{iπ} + 1 = 0.

column1: What are the nth roots of unity, and what do they look like geometrically?
column2: The solutions of zⁿ = 1: z = e^{2πik/n} for k = 0, 1, …, n − 1. They are n equally spaced points on the unit circle |z| = 1.

column1: State the Cauchy–Riemann equations for f = u + iv with z = x + iy.
column2: If f is holomorphic then uₓ = v_y and u_y = −vₓ (partial derivatives). Together with continuous partials, these imply f is holomorphic.

column1: Define what it means for a function to be analytic (holomorphic) on an open set.
column2: f is holomorphic on an open set U if it is complex-differentiable at every point of U, i.e. the limit (f(z+h) − f(z))/h exists as h → 0 (h complex) for each z ∈ U.

column1: State Cauchy's integral theorem.
column2: If f is holomorphic on a simply connected open set and γ is a closed contour in it, then ∮_γ f(z) dz = 0.

column1: State Cauchy's integral formula.
column2: If f is holomorphic on and inside a positively oriented simple closed contour γ, and a is interior to γ, then f(a) = (1/(2πi)) ∮_γ f(z)/(z − a) dz.

column1: State the residue theorem.
column2: If f is holomorphic inside and on a positively oriented simple closed contour γ except at isolated singularities zₖ, then ∮_γ f(z) dz = 2πi Σ Res(f, zₖ).

column1: How do you compute the residue at a simple pole z₀ of f?
column2: Res(f, z₀) = lim(z→z₀) (z − z₀) f(z). For f = g/h with simple zero of h at z₀, this equals g(z₀)/h′(z₀).

column1: For z = x + iy, give the complex conjugate z̄ and the identity relating z, z̄, and |z|.
column2: z̄ = x − iy. Then z·z̄ = x² + y² = |z|², so |z| = √(z z̄).

column1: Under the map w = e^z, what is the image of a horizontal line y = c (c constant) in the z-plane?
column2: Writing z = x + ic, w = e^x e^{ic}, so as x ranges over ℝ, w traces the ray from the origin (excluding 0) at angle c: arg(w) = c, |w| = e^x > 0.

column1: What is a sample space?
column2: The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment, usually denoted Ω or S.

column1: State the three Kolmogorov axioms of probability.
column2: 1) P(A) ≥ 0 for any event A; 2) P(Ω) = 1; 3) for disjoint events, P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B).

column1: Define the conditional probability P(A | B).
column2: P(A | B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B), provided P(B) > 0.

column1: State Bayes' theorem.
column2: P(A | B) = P(B | A) · P(A) / P(B), where P(B) > 0.

column1: When are two events A and B independent?
column2: When P(A ∩ B) = P(A) · P(B); equivalently P(A | B) = P(A).

column1: State the law of total probability for a partition B₁,...,Bₙ of the sample space.
column2: P(A) = Σ P(A | Bᵢ) · P(Bᵢ) summed over i = 1 to n.

column1: State the linearity of expectation for random variables X and Y.
column2: E[aX + bY] = a·E[X] + b·E[Y], which holds even if X and Y are not independent.

column1: Define the variance of a random variable X.
column2: Var(X) = E[(X − μ)²] = E[X²] − (E[X])², where μ = E[X].

column1: What are the mean and variance of a Binomial(n, p) distribution?
column2: Mean = np and variance = np(1 − p).

column1: State the 68-95-99.7 rule for a normal distribution.
column2: About 68% of values lie within 1σ of μ, 95% within 2σ, and 99.7% within 3σ.

column1: What are the mean and variance of a Poisson(λ) distribution?
column2: Both the mean and the variance equal λ.

column1: For a geometric distribution with success probability p (trials until first success), what is the expected number of trials?
column2: E[X] = 1/p, and variance = (1 − p)/p².

column1: When rolling two fair dice, what is P(sum = 7)?
column2: 1/6, since 6 of the 36 equally likely outcomes sum to 7.

column1: What is the mean of a continuous Uniform(a, b) distribution?
column2: (a + b)/2, and its variance is (b − a)²/12.

column1: State the fundamental counting (multiplication) principle.
column2: If one task can be done in m ways and another in n ways, the two together can be done in m · n ways.

column1: How many permutations (orderings) are there of n distinct objects?
Give the formula for combinations C(n, k).
column2: C(n, k) = n! / (k! (n − k)!), the number of k-element subsets of an n-set.

column1: State the binomial theorem.
column2: (x + y)ⁿ = Σ from k=0 to n of C(n, k) · xⁿ⁻ᵏ · yᵏ.

column1: State the pigeonhole principle.
column2: If n items are placed into m boxes with n > m, then at least one box contains at least two items.

column1: State the inclusion-exclusion principle for two sets.
State the handshaking lemma (degree sum formula) for a graph.
column2: The sum of all vertex degrees equals 2|E|, twice the number of edges.

column1: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?
column2: Exactly n − 1 edges.

column1: State Euler's formula for a connected planar graph.
column2: V − E + F = 2, where V, E, F are the numbers of vertices, edges, and faces.

column1: How many subsets does a set with n elements have?
column2: 2ⁿ, including the empty set and the set itself.

column1: Describe the idea of floating-point round-off error.
column2: Real numbers are stored with finite precision, so most values are rounded to the nearest representable number, introducing a relative error bounded by machine epsilon.

column1: State the iteration formula for Newton's method.
Describe the bisection method and its convergence.
column2: On [a, b] with f(a)·f(b) < 0, repeatedly halve the interval keeping the sign change; the root is bracketed and error halves each step (linear convergence).

column1: What is the fixed-point iteration for solving x = g(x)?
column2: x_{n+1} = g(x_n); it converges near a fixed point x* if |g'(x*)| < 1.

column1: State the trapezoidal rule for ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx (single interval) and its error order.
column2: ≈ (b − a)/2 · (f(a) + f(b)); the composite error is O(h²).

column1: State Simpson's rule for ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx and its error order.
column2: ≈ (b − a)/6 · (f(a) + 4f((a+b)/2) + f(b)); the composite error is O(h⁴).

column1: Give the linear interpolation estimate of f(x) between points (x₀, y₀) and (x₁, y₁).
column2: f(x) ≈ y₀ + (y₁ − y₀)/(x₁ − x₀) · (x − x₀).

column1: What does the condition number of a problem measure?
column2: How sensitive the output is to small changes in the input; a large condition number means the problem is ill-conditioned.

column1: Define the order of convergence p of a sequence converging to x*.
column2: It is the value p for which |x_{n+1} − x*| ≈ C·|x_n − x*|ᵖ; p = 1 is linear, p = 2 is quadratic (e.g., Newton's method).
